title: Mountain Wall Hooks
summary: A mountain-silhouette plywood plaque with dowel pegs for hooks — a quick, cute wall rack for a kid's room or entry, made from a scrap of plywood and a dowel.
description:
This is a charming afternoon project: a plywood board cut into a mountain-range silhouette with a few dowel pegs poking out as hooks. It's the kind of build that looks designer but costs a scrap of 3/4" plywood and a length of dowel, and it's a great introduction to jigsaw shaping and clean dowel joinery.

The mountain shape is just straight lines between marked points, so it's forgiving — mark the peaks and valleys on the top and bottom edges, connect them, and cut to the lines with a jigsaw. There's no curve to fair, which makes it beginner-friendly.

The pegs are where a little precision pays off. Drill a small pilot hole all the way through the plywood at each peg location, then use a Forstner bit centered on that pilot to bore a flat-bottomed socket 3/8" deep — the pilot keeps the Forstner centered and gives you a screw path. Glue the dowels into the sockets, then flip the board and drive a self-tapping screw through the back into each dowel so a coat or bag can't pull it loose. Finish, add a picture-hanging kit, and hang it level.

tools.note: Cut the plywood blank to size.
Flat-bottomed sockets for the dowel pegs; size to your dowel.
Pilot holes to center the Forstner and to screw the dowels.

materials.name: Wood screws, 1-1/4" and 2" self-tapping
materials.note: Through the back into each dowel.

cutList.note: Cut the mountain silhouette from straight lines between marked points.
Round stock; thickness and width are the diameter. Cut from the 10" length.

steps.title: Cut the mountain shape
steps.body: Cut the plywood to size with a circular saw. Mark the peak and valley points on the top and bottom edges, draw straight lines between them, and cut out the mountain silhouette with a jigsaw following the lines. Sand the edges smooth.

steps.title: Bore the peg sockets
steps.body: Mark the dowel locations. Drill a small pilot hole all the way through the plywood at each, then use the Forstner bit centered on that pilot to drill a flat-bottomed socket 3/8" deep. The pilot keeps the Forstner centered and gives you a screw path for later.

steps.title: Set the pegs and hang
steps.body: Cut the dowels to length (a miter saw is cleanest; a jigsaw or circular saw works if you clamp them first). Glue them into the sockets, then flip the board and drive a self-tapping screw through the back into each dowel so a load can't pull it out. Finish the piece, add a picture-hanging kit, and hang it level.

images.alt: Mountain-shaped plywood wall rack with dowel peg hooks
